stories.headline: US-Iran deal nears a Geneva signing as a standoff over Iran's enriched uranium clouds the terms; Israel says it is not a party
stories.summary: A Western source told Reuters the US-Iran memorandum to halt the Gulf war could be signed as soon as Sunday in Geneva — reportedly by VP JD Vance and Iran's parliament speaker — waiving oil sanctions, unfreezing funds and halting hostilities. But the fate of Iran's near-weapons-grade uranium is unresolved: Trump says it must be removed or seized 'by force,' while Iranian sources say Tehran will keep the stockpile at home. Iran's foreign ministry called a finalized deal 'merely speculation.'

perspectives.keyFraming: A memorandum could be signed within days, but key approvals and the hardest nuclear questions remain unresolved.
perspectives.summary: Reuters reported the MoU could be signed as soon as Sunday in Geneva — reportedly by VP Vance and Iran's parliament speaker Qalibaf — waiving oil sanctions, releasing frozen funds and requiring a halt to hostilities on all fronts including Lebanon, with the Strait of Hormuz to reopen with no transit charges within 30 days. Trump said he canceled fresh strikes because the US had 'made a great settlement,' but reported terms appeared to favour Tehran and Iran said no final decision had been made.

sources.title: Iran peace memorandum could be signed on Sunday in Geneva, source says
U.S. and Iran signal peace deal close as reports suggest terms appear to favour Tehran

perspectives.keyFraming: Israel publicly distances itself while the central dispute — removing Iran's highly enriched uranium — stays unresolved.
perspectives.summary: The Times of Israel reported a senior US official saying the pending deal 'leads to' the US obtaining Iran's enriched material, while Trump conceded the MoU only 'conceptually' addresses the nuclear file. Netanyahu's office said Israel is not a party to the deal, and Iranian sources said Tehran had decided to keep its near-weapons-grade uranium inside the country, defying a core US demand.

sources.title: Iranian sources say regime has decided to keep highly enriched uranium in Iran, defying Trump

perspectives.keyFraming: Tehran insists no deal is final and that any agreement must also end the fighting in Lebanon.
perspectives.summary: Foreign Minister Araghchi, speaking via Iranian state media, said the 'Islamabad Memorandum has never been closer' but is unsigned and could still change, describing a two-stage MoU and urging media to stop treating it as finalized. Iran's foreign ministry earlier called reports of a finalized agreement 'merely speculation' and said any deal must halt hostilities in Lebanon as well; officials reiterated they would not ship near-weapons-grade uranium abroad.

sources.title: Iran says no final decision made on deal Trump hopes could be signed soon
Iran's FM says ceasefire deal with US has 'never been closer'

stories.headline: US government orders Anthropic to suspend worldwide access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models over national-security directive
stories.summary: Anthropic shut off global access to its newly launched Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 12 after a US export-control directive — reportedly a letter from Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick to CEO Dario Amodei — citing a reported jailbreak. The models had launched only three days earlier on June 9, and all other Claude models are unaffected. It appears to be the first government-forced takedown of a publicly deployed frontier AI model.

perspectives.keyFraming: Reporting frames the directive as an unprecedented state intervention in a deployed frontier model.
perspectives.summary: TechRadar and The New Stack reported that a June 12 US directive forced Anthropic to shut off worldwide access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5 just three days after launch, citing a potential jailbreak. The order reportedly bars foreign nationals — including foreign Anthropic staff in the US — from access, and outlets describe it as a watershed for AI national-security policy.

sources.title: After a 'potential jailbreak', Anthropic is shutting off access to its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models under national security orders from the US government
US government forces shutdown of Anthropic's AI Fable 5 and Mythos 5

perspectives.keyFraming: Anthropic says the underlying jailbreak was narrow and the surfaced vulnerabilities minor, but is complying with the order.
perspectives.summary: In its own statement, Anthropic said it received only verbal evidence of a narrow, non-universal jailbreak — asking the model to read a codebase and fix flaws — and that the surfaced vulnerabilities were minor and already publicly known. The company said it is complying with the government directive while disputing the severity of the reported issue.

sources.title: Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
Federal government orders Anthropic to pull Fable 5 and Mythos 5, three days after launch

stories.headline: Federal judge indefinitely blocks Trump's $1.8B 'anti-weaponization' fund, demands sworn declaration it is dead
stories.summary: US District Judge Leonie Brinkema issued a preliminary injunction on June 12 indefinitely barring the Trump administration from creating the roughly $1.8 billion 'anti-weaponization' fund that grew out of a settlement of Trump's lawsuit against the IRS and DOJ. She said she did not believe the fund was truly dead because DOJ's assurances were not made under penalty of perjury, and gave the administration a week to swear it will not revive it under a new name.

perspectives.keyFraming: The court treats the administration's assurances as legally insufficient and demands accountability under oath.
perspectives.summary: NBC News and The Hill reported the judge indefinitely blocked the fund and ordered a sworn declaration, saying DOJ statements that the program was dead carried no legal weight without being made under penalty of perjury. The ruling extends an earlier temporary order while the underlying challenge proceeds.

sources.title: Federal judge indefinitely blocks Trump's 'anti-weaponization' fund

perspectives.keyFraming: Coverage frames the fund as an attempt to channel a self-dealing settlement and casts the judge as a check on it.
perspectives.summary: CNN reported that Brinkema said she did not believe the fund was dead and extended her order blocking it, pressing the administration to prove in a sworn filing that the money would not be redirected under a different name.

sources.title: Judge says she doesn't believe 'anti-weaponization' fund is dead; extends order blocking it

perspectives.keyFraming: Conservative coverage casts the order as a Clinton-appointed judge overreaching against the administration.
perspectives.summary: Fox News reported the judge warned the administration against 'playing possum' with the fund, framing the indefinite block and the demand for a sworn statement as an aggressive intervention by the court into an executive-branch settlement.

sources.title: Clinton judge warns Trump DOJ against 'playing possum' with anti-weaponization fund

stories.headline: US stocks close higher and notch a winning week as Iran peace hopes pull oil to a two-month low
stories.summary: US indexes finished June 12 higher — the Dow up about 0.7% near 51,200, the S&P 500 up roughly 0.5% and the Nasdaq up about 0.3% — as Wall Street bet on a near-term US-Iran deal that would lift oil sanctions and reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Crude fell more than 4% to a two-month low below $85 WTI. Breadth was strong even as mega-cap growth names slipped, and the S&P booked a gain for the week ahead of the June 16-17 FOMC.

perspectives.keyFraming: Risk-on into the close on de-escalation and cooling oil carried a broad-based advance.
perspectives.summary: TheStreet and Schwab framed the session as a relief rally, with peace optimism and a 2-4% drop in crude powering gains across cyclicals and the equal-weight index, leaving the S&P on track for a positive week even as the largest growth names eased.

sources.title: Stock Market Today (June 12, 2026): SpaceX debuts as indexes rise on a possible Iran deal
Stocks on track for a positive week amid peace hopes

perspectives.keyFraming: Falling energy prices are the real tailwind, easing the inflation overhang into next week's Fed decision.
perspectives.summary: Markets coverage centered on cheaper oil relieving inflation pressure ahead of the June 16-17 FOMC, with traders pricing essentially no change in rates despite back-to-back hot CPI and PPI prints, as the geopolitical risk premium unwound.

sources.title: Dow, S&P 500 and Nasdaq forecasts: oil, peace hopes and the Fed

stories.headline: Hamas accuses Israel of shifting Gaza's 'yellow line' westward to derail Cairo ceasefire talks as strikes continue
stories.summary: Hamas said Israel's continued westward movement of the Gaza 'yellow line' — the withdrawal boundary set under the first phase of the US ceasefire plan — and its expansion of control over Palestinian land aim to 'blow up the negotiation track' as factions and mediators met in Cairo. Israeli strikes killed several people in Gaza on June 12, with reports the army had pushed the line about 300 metres west in several areas over the past week.

perspectives.keyFraming: Hamas frames the line-shifting as a deliberate Israeli effort to sabotage a fragile US-brokered ceasefire.
perspectives.summary: The Express Tribune reported Hamas accusing Israel of moving the 'yellow line' westward and seizing more land to 'blow up the negotiation track' as talks in Cairo with Egyptian, Qatari and Turkish mediators sought to advance the next phase of the ceasefire.

sources.title: Israel expanding Gaza 'yellow line' to derail ceasefire talks, says Hamas

perspectives.keyFraming: Mediators struggle to consolidate a fragile truce as Israeli strikes keep killing Palestinians.
perspectives.summary: CBC reported that an Israeli drone strike killed people in Gaza as the ceasefire standoff continued, with mediators in Cairo trying to move to the next phase of the US plan amid disputes over Israeli troop positions and the pace of withdrawal.

sources.title: Israeli drone strike kills 2 people in Gaza as ceasefire standoff continues
